coredata.dc:title: Multi-functional carbon dots-based nanoprobe for ratiometric enzyme reaction monitoring and biothiol analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ya
Xu, Jie
Lei, Li
Wang, Fengyang
Xu, Zhiai
Zhang, Wen
coredata.dc:description: In this work, red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through microwave-assisted heating and then conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) to construct a ratiometric multi-functional nanoprobe (CDs-F) for both pH sensing and biothiols analysis. After coupling with FITC, the as-obtained CDs-F shows both green and red emissions. Based on the pH sensitivity of FITC, the CDs-F can be used for ratiometric pH sensing. On the other hand, the red emission of CDs can be quenched significantly by Ag+. Studies of quenching mechanism demonstrate that Ag+ could coordinate with the functional groups of CDs resulting in the formation of the CDs-Ag+ complex and also charge transfer between the CDs and Ag+. Simultaneous static and dynamic quenching processes impart the maximum quenching. Based on the strong affinity between thiol groups and Ag+, the CDs-F with dual emissions can be used for ratiometric biothiols analysis with the detection limits of tens of nanomole per liter. Furthermore, the strategy has been successfully applied to monitor proton-related enzymatic catalytic reactions and to determine biothiols in fetal bovine serum samples with good recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Ratiometric nanoprobe
pH
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of the CDs and CDs-F
Ratiometric pH sensing and enzyme reactions monitoring by CDs-F
Ratiometric biothiols sensing by combining CDs-F with Ag+
Characterizations and properties of the CDs
Ratiometric pH sensing and enzyme reactions monitoring by CDs-F
The quenching of the CDs fluorescence by Ag+
Ratiometric biothiols sensing by combining CDs-F with Ag+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the CDs and the construction of ratiometric nanoprobe for pH and biothiols analysis.

(A) TEM image and the size distributions, (B) HR-TEM image of the CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum with emission at 680 nm, fluorescence emission spectrum with excitation at 400 nm of the CDs. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths from 360 to 640 nm.
UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence emission spectra under excitation of 400 and 490 nm of the CDs-F.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs (10 μg/mL) in BR buffer with pH from 4.5 to 9.0. (B) Fluorescence intensity at 680, 650 nm (F680, F650) versus pH varying. Excitation wavelength: 400 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-F (5 μg/mL) in BR buffer with pH from 5.0 to 8.2. (B) The plot of F518/F680 versus pH varying. F518 is the fluorescence intensity at 518 nm excited at 490 nm, and F680 is the fluorescence intensity at 680 nm excited at 400 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-F (5 μg/mL) for monitoring glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx (0.1 mg/mL) from 0 to 100 μM in a 0.02 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). (B) Plot of F518/F680 versus the concentration of glucose and fitting data. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs-F for monitoring the hydrolysis of urea catalyzed by urease (0.5 mg/mL) from 0 to 1 mM in a 0.02 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). (D) Plot of F518/F680 versus the concentration of urea and fitting data. Excitation wavelength: 400 and 490 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of different concentrations of Ag+ from 0 to 50 μM with excitation of 400 nm. (B) The relationship between F680 and the concentrations of Ag+. Inset: The correlation between F0/F and the concentrations of Ag+, F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 680 nm in the absence and presence of Ag+. (C) UV–vis spectra of the CDs with addition of Ag+. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of the CDs, CDs-Ag+, CDs-Ag+-Cys. The concentrations of Ag+ and Cys are 40 μM and 60 μM respectively. The black line is the instrument response.

The responses of CDs-F-Ag+ complex to various concentrations of (A, B) Cys, (C, D) GSH, (E, F) Hcy and corresponding linear relationships between the ratio of F680/F518 and the concentrations of biothiols. [Ag+]: 4 μM, CDs-F: 5 μg/mL, excitation wavelength: 400 nm.
Detection of Cys in 1% FBS with the CDs-F-Ag+. [Ag+]: 4 μM. CDs-F: 5 μg/mL.

head.ce:title: Multi-functional carbon dots-based nanoprobe for ratiometric enzyme reaction monitoring and biothiol analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200241, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through microwave-assisted heating and then conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) to construct a ratiometric multi-functional nanoprobe (CDs-F) for both pH sensing and biothiols analysis. After coupling with FITC, the as-obtained CDs-F shows both green and red emissions. Based on the pH sensitivity of FITC, the CDs-F can be used for ratiometric pH sensing. On the other hand, the red emission of CDs can be quenched significantly by Ag+. Studies of quenching mechanism demonstrate that Ag+ could coordinate with the functional groups of CDs resulting in the formation of the CDs-Ag+ complex and also charge transfer between the CDs and Ag+. Simultaneous static and dynamic quenching processes impart the maximum quenching. Based on the strong affinity between thiol groups and Ag+, the CDs-F with dual emissions can be used for ratiometric biothiols analysis with the detection limits of tens of nanomole per liter. Furthermore, the strategy has been successfully applied to monitor proton-related enzymatic catalytic reactions and to determine biothiols in fetal bovine serum samples with good recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: As a newly emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered great attention in biosensing, bioimaging, biomedical and opto-electronic fields for the past few years due to their fascinating properties [1–8]. As compared to traditional organic dyes, CDs exhibit improved photostability, water dispersibility and biocompatibility [9–12]. And compared with semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) composed of toxic heavy metal elements, CDs show lower toxicity which is favorable for biological applications [13–16]. Although numerous synthetic methods have been reported to obtain various CDs, most of which show blue to green emissions [17–20], therefore, it is still highly desirable to prepare CDs with remarkable fluorescence property of long-wavelength emission especially, aqueous solubility and easy functionality through simpler approaches for both biosensing and imaging applications.

Recently, ratiometric optical analysis has become a research hotspot because the measurement accuracy can be improved significantly through the evaluation of the ratio of signal intensities under two distinct channels [21–23]. So far, most ratiometric fluorescent probes are usually constructed by combining two fluorophores of different emissions together. The common components include synthetic small-molecule fluorescent probes [24] and various kinds of emissive nanomaterials, such as carbon dots [25], semiconductor quantum dots [25,26], metal nanoclusters [27], upconversion materials [28], etc. Among them, CDs have obtained more attention due to their distinctive optical properties. However, most of these ratiometric probes are for only one specific target. It is meaningful and challenging to design novel fluorescent probes for ratiometric sensing of two or more different targets simultaneously which could provide more comprehensive information for chemical and biological analysis [29–31].

Herein, a CDs-based ratiometric nanoprobe (CDs-F) for both pH sensing and biothiols analysis was constructed by combining the red-emissive CDs with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC). As is well-known, pH is an important parameter in all kinds of biological processes and a small fluctuation of pH can disturb the kinetics of a biochemical reaction [32]. So the accurate detection of pH is meaningful and the ratiometric strategy can improve the measurement accuracy. Meanwhile, biothiols, as a type of reactive sulfur species with free thiol group, play essential roles in maintaining cell functions [33–35]. As three representative biothiols, cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (Hcy) have been the research focus because their abnormal levels are closely related to various impairments [36–39]. Several strategies based on CDs have been developed for biothiols determination [40–42]. In this work, the conjugated CDs-F could sense pH and biothiols in a ratiometric mode respectively as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The initial CDs prepared by microwave assisted heating show stable red emission over a certain pH range while it can be quenched significantly by Ag+. Based on the pH sensitivity of FITC and the strong affinity between thiol groups and Ag+, the CDs-F with dual emissions can be used for ratiometric pH sensing and biothiols analysis respectively. The results demonstrate that the ratiometric probe could detect biothiols with the detection limits (LOD) of tens of nanomole per liter and realize the differentiation of pH variation from 5 to 8. Furthermore, the strategy has been successfully applied to monitor proton-related enzymatic catalytic reactions and to determine biothiols in fetal bovine serum (FBS) samples.

GSH, Cys, Hcy, FITC, AgNO3, 5, 5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), HEPES, 3,3′-dithiodipropionic acid (DTDPA), 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA), 4-nitrothiophenol (NTP), glucose oxidase (GOx), urease were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich China (Shanghai, China). NaF, NaCl, KBr, KI, NaAc, NaH2PO4, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic Acid (Mes), ammonium 1-pyrrolidinedithiocarbamate (APDC), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MH), H3BO3, H3PO4, glucose, urea were obtained from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Formamide was purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). l-leucine (Leu), l-isoleucine (Ile), β-alanine (Ala), l-serine (Ser), l-threonine (Thr), l-asparagine (Asn), l-valine (Val), l-phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), l-aspartic acid (Asp), l-glutamic Acid (Glu), l-proline (Pro), l-glutamine (Gln), methionine (Met), Na2S, 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA), Thioflavine S (ThS), lipoic acid, thiamazole were purchased from J&K Chemicals (Beijing, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water deionized by Hitech laboratory water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm).

MDS-8 microwave chemical reactor (Sineo Microwave Chemistry Technology, Shanghai) was used to prepare the CDs. Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM) images were acquired with JEM-2100F microscopy (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was measured by a Thermo Nicolet 380 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were collected by a ESCALab 220-Xi (VG, UK). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained on an X’Pert Philips Materials Research Diffractometer (Brook AXS, Germany). Absorption spectra were acquired with a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). Fluorescence spectra were collected with a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA) at room temperature. The time-resolved fluorescence decay was determined with Edinburgh Instruments Model FLS980 (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of the CDs and CDs-F
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared according to a previous report [43]. Specifically, 0.68 g GSH was dissolved in 20 mL formamide. The solution was then transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in a microwave chemical reactor at 160 °C for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with water and then purified by dialysis (1000 Da of MWCO) for 3 days. The obtained green solution was filtered through 0.22 μm Millipore membrane followed by freeze-drying.

The CDs (10 mg) and FITC (1.5 mg) were dissolved in 15 mL NaHCO3 solution (0.1 M) in a round bottomed flask. After stirring for 24 h, the mixture was subjected to dialysis with tube of MWCO 1000. The unreacted FITC was removed by using 0.1 M NaHCO3 solution for dialysis on the first day and then deionized water was used in the next two days followed by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric pH sensing and enzyme reactions monitoring by CDs-F
ce:section.ce:para: All the sensing experiments were carried out in BR buffer (10 mM). The final concentration of the CDs-F is 5.0 μg/mL. The fluorescence spectra were obtained with the excitation wavelength of 400 and 490 nm.

To demonstrate the potential of the CDs-F for enzyme reactions monitoring, the concentrations of GOx and urease were fixed at 0.1 and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. Upon addition of the substrates, the mixtures were incubated at 37 °C (25 °C for urease) for 1 h to complete the catalytic reactions. Afterwards, the CDs-F was added as the fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric biothiols sensing by combining CDs-F with Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: All the sensing experiments were carried out in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4). To study the quenching of the CDs by Ag+, the CDs were mixed with Ag+ of different concentrations and incubated for 5 min. Then, the fluorescence spectra, absorption spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra were recorded, respectively. The excitation wavelength for fluorescence measurements was 400 nm. For the effect of temperature on the quenching, 10, 37, 50 °C were chosen for comparison. For the detection of biothiols in buffer, the CDs or CDs-F were first mixed with a certain concentration of Ag+. After incubation for 5 min, various concentrations of biothiols (Cys, GSH, Hcy) were added followed by reacting for another 10 min. The fluorescence spectra were obtained with the excitation wavelength at 400 nm. For the study of selectivity, amino acids without thiol group, common anions, and other sulfur-containing compounds were added into the system. When it comes to the real samples, FBS samples were first treated to remove proteins. In brief, 0.5 mL of acetonitrile was added into a 0.5 mL FBS sample, and then the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was diluted for further detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and properties of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared by facile microwave-assisted heating and can be readily dispersed in water which could keep stable for several months. TEM results show that the CDs are well-dispersed exhibiting size distribution between 2.2–3.7 nm as calculated by counting several hundred particles (Fig. 1 A). A few particles show the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1B) corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite but most others are amorphous as confirmed by the XRD pattern (Fig. S1A) [3,31]. FTIR and XPS results (Fig. S1B, Fig. S2A–E) together confirm that the resultant CDs contain abundant functional groups such as OH, NH2, COOH, CONH, etc., which lays the foundation for further modification and construction of sensing platform [43].

The CDs have several dominant absorption bands in the range of 200–800 nm (Fig. 1C) ascribed to electron transitions of the aromatic system with CO/CN/CS bonds [43]. Fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1D show excitation-independent behavior of the CDs with maximum emission at 680 nm and the optimum excitation at 400 nm. Additionally, the photostability measurement indicates that the CDs own good photostability which would be favorable for their application (Fig. S2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric pH sensing and enzyme reactions monitoring by CDs-F
ce:section.ce:para: FITC was conjugated with the CDs to be a pH-responsive unit. From FTIR spectra in Fig. S3A, it is observed that the peak of NC = S at 2050 cm−1 for CDs-F disappears and the peak at 1160 cm−1 increases indicating the formation of thiourea group [43]. Zeta potential of the CDs-F decreases from −12.0 mV of the CDs to −34.7 mV due to the consumption of the amino group on the surface of CDs (Fig. S3B). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs-F exhibits an obvious absorption peak at 490 nm which is the characteristic peak of FITC (Fig. 2 ), demonstrating the successful combination of the CDs with FITC. As-obtained CDs-F show both green and red emissions with maximum emissions at 518 nm and 680 nm, respectively (Fig. 2).

The effect of pH on the emission of the CDs was first tested as shown in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity at 680 nm (F680) of the CDs remains almost unchanged in the pH range of 4.5–7.4. And then the emission at 680 nm gradually shifts to 650 nm with increasing pH to 9 owing to the fact that more structures of NHCO in the CDs are converted into the form of NCOH which is consistent with previous report [43]. The fluorescence stability of the CDs in a certain range of pH endows the CDs with potential to be a reference in a ratiometric pH sensor.

The ratiometric pH analysis with the CDs-F was first performed in Britton–Robinson (BR) universal buffer. As shown in Fig. 4 A, as the pH increases from 5.0 to 8.2, the fluorescence intensity at 518 nm (F518) increases gradually while F680 keeps the same. Fig. 4B shows the plot of F518/F680 versus pH values. It can be seen that the value of F518/F680 is in a good linear relationship with pH in the range of 5.8-7.8 confirming the ratiometric pH sensing property of the CDs-F.

The application of the CDs-F as a ratiometric pH sensor was further demonstrated by monitoring enzyme catalytic reactions. We chose two typical enzymatic reactions as examples: (1) GOx catalyzes glucose and oxygen leading to the generation of gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide and (2) urease catalyzes the hydrolysis of urea to produce ammonia and CO2. Both these two reactions would lead to the changes of pH and therefore be detected by the CDs-F. As displayed in Fig. 5 A and B, with increasing glucose concentration from 0 to 100 μM, the ratio of F518/F680 decreases accordingly indicating the decline of solution pH induced by the enzyme reaction. In contrast, in the reaction of urease, by varying the concentration of the substrate urea from 0 to 1000 μM, the value of F518/F680 increases consequently, revealing the consumption of proton and pH increase (Fig. 5C and D). We calculated the apparent Michaelis–Menten constants (K m) of these two reactions in our systems from the Lineweaver–Burk equation. K m of 0.062 mM and 0.154 mM were obtained for GOx and urease respectively, which are comparable to previously published values [44,45], indicating that the addition of the CDs-F does not affect the affinity of the enzymes to the relevant substrates. Therefore, the CDs-F can be utilized as the ratiometric probe to monitor those proton-relative enzyme catalytic reactions effectively and to detect the corresponding enzyme substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching of the CDs fluorescence by Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Given the abundant functional groups on the surface, the CDs are expected to be susceptible to some external stimulation. Accordingly, effects of various metal ions on the fluorescence of the CDs were investigated as shown in Fig. S4. Obvious quenching of the CDs emission was observed for several metal ions especially for Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ag+ ions, probably originated from the interactions between the metal ions and the surface groups of the CDs. Interestingly, it was found that only the fluorescence of the CDs-Ag+ system recovered upon addition of Cys possibly due to the higher affinity of other metal ions to the CDs than to Cys (Fig. S4). So we thought the CDs-Ag+ complex would be a potential system for biothiols analysis.

Thus a series of studies about quenching of the CDs fluorescence by Ag+ were carried out in succession. As shown in Fig. 6 A and B, F680 of the CDs gradually decreases with increasing the concentration of Ag+ from 1 to 50 μM and finally reaches the minimum (close to zero). It indicates that the emission of the CDs can be almost totally quenched by Ag+, providing a lower background for succeeding sensing. Generally, the quenching of the fluorophores can be realized through dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or static quenching effect (SQE) or both of them. Both models can be described in theory by Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1 + K sv[Q]
Where F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophore in the absence and presence of the quencher respectively; [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher and K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [41]. In this work, the correlation between F0/F and the concentration of Ag+ is illustrated in the inset of Fig. 6B which exhibits an upward-sloping curve instead of a simple linear relation, suggesting that more than one type of the quenching model (DQE or SQE) are involved in this system simultaneously.

To further demonstrate the quenching mechanism, the absorption behavior, fluorescence lifetime decays and temperature-dependent property were then studied. It is obvious that with the addition of Ag+, the absorption peaks of the CDs at 400 and 620 nm generate gradual red shifts, indicating the formation of new complex between the CDs and Ag+ and the occurrence of SQE (Fig. 6C). The fluorescence decay tests in Fig. 6D and Table S1 show that the average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs-Ag+ decreases to 2.91 ns from 4.92 ns of the CDs alone, implying a fast charge transfer and DQE process [46]. In addition, the variation of temperature doesn’t cause evident change of the quenching constant and similar correlations between F0/F and the concentration of Ag+ are observed as shown in Fig. S5. This could result from the counterbalance of the opposite effect of the temperature on DQE and SQE. Based on the above results, it is proposed that the CDs can be quenched by Ag+ through both SQE and DQE process. Ag+ ions first coordinate with the functional groups of CDs (-NH2, etc.) resulting in the formation of the CDs-Ag+ complex and also fast charge transfer between the CDs and Ag+, that is, SQE and DQE existed simultaneously and together realize the maximum quenching of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric biothiols sensing by combining CDs-F with Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that thiol groups have strong affinity to Ag+ [40,47]. So the above CDs-Ag+ system could be a potential platform for turn-on fluorescence detection of biothiols. The feasibility has been confirmed as described in Fig. 6D and Fig. S4. The responses of CDs-Ag+ complex to Cys, GSH, Hcy show similar concentration-dependent manner (Fig. S6). The experiment of pH effect on the response indicates that pH in the range of 6.8–7.8 has no great influence, so pH of 7.4 was chosen (Fig. S7). The concentration of the CDs was optimized firstly and the results were shown in Fig. S8–S10 and Table S2. We can see that the CDs with lower concentrations show lower LOD while the CDs with higher concentrations display wider response range. In addition, selectivity test was performed subsequently (Fig. S11). Sulfur-free amino acids, most of common anions, and sulfur-contained compounds without thiol group could not cause obvious change of the fluorescence of CDs-Ag+ complex. However, I−, S2−, and other free thiol group-contained compounds such as thiamazole, MH, NTP, APDC, MPBA, MUA could also induce the fluorescence recovery of the CDs-Ag+ complex in varying degrees. Free I− and S2− would react with Ag+ to form insoluble compounds with low K sp. The strategy using CDs-Ag+ complex to detect biothiols is based on the strong affinity of thiol groups to Ag+, so we can use the strategy to detect biothiols level in pretreated FBS samples free of underlying interference.

Then the CDs-F probe was used for ratiometric sensing of biothiols. To acquire good sensing performance for biothiols, the concentration of the CDs-F (1.25, 5, 10 μg/mL) was optimized firstly as shown in Fig. 7 , Fig. S12 and S13 and Table S3. Taken both LOD and linear range into consideration, 5 μg/mL of CDs-F and 4 μM of Ag+ were selected for the following ratiometric sensing of biothiols. As shown in Fig. 7, different concentrations (0–10 μM) of biothiols (Cys, GSH, Hcy) are added into the CDs-F-Ag+ system respectively, and corresponding ratios of F680/F518 increase with enhancing the concentrations of biothiols and finally reach to a plateau. Good linear relationships in the concentration ranges of 1.2–4.5, 1.2–6.0, 1.5–6.0 μM for Cys, GSH, Hcy respectively are obtained and the respective LOD are 55, 59, 68 nM (3σ/slope of the calibration curve). Besides, the results of selectivity measurements are in agreement with those of the CDs (Fig. S14).

The application of the CDs-F as a ratiometric biothiols sensor was further demonstrated by FBS sample detection. After simple treatment of FBS to remove proteins, FBS samples with 100-fold dilution are subjected to the sensing system. Calculated from the standard curve in buffer (Fig. 7), the concentration of biothiols in 1% FBS is 1.25 μM, which is well-consistent with the standard Ellman method. Further standard addition experiments have also obtained satisfactory recoveries (Table 1 ).

In summary, red-emissive CDs were prepared through a facile microwave-assisted heating method and the CDs-based ratiometric nanoprobe CDs-F was then constructed for both pH and biothiols analysis. The CDs-F shows dual green and red emissions which response to pH and Ag+ respectively. Based on the pH sensitivity of FITC and the strong affinity between thiol groups and Ag+, the CDs-F was utilized for ratiomertic pH and biothiols sensing. The results indicate that pH variation from 5.8 to 7.8 could be differentiated in such a ratiometric mode and the LOD of tens of nanomole per liter for biothiols was realized by using this probe. Furthermore, the strategy has been successfully applied to moniter proton-related enzymatic catalytic reactions and to determine biothiol in FBS sample.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775046, 21675055, 21635003) are greatly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.183.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
A tunable ratiometric pH sensor based on carbon nanodots for the quantitative measurement of the intracellular pH of whole cells
Multi-doped carbon dots with ratiometric pH sensing properties for monitoring enzyme catalytic reactions
Self-targeting fluorescent carbon dots for diagnosis of brain cancer cells
A fluorescent carbon-dots-based mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe for peroxynitrite sensing in living cells
Hydrophobic-carbon-dot-based dual-emission micelle for ratiometric fluorescence biosensing and imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells
Carbon and graphene quantum dots for optoelectronic and energy devices: a review
Three colors emission from S, N Co-doped graphene quantum dots for visible light H2 production and bioimaging
Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection
Germanium-doped carbon dots as a new type of fluorescent probe for visualizing the dynamic invasions of mercury (II) ions into cancer cells
In situ growth of surfactant-free gold nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide in biological environments
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials
Super-resolution fluorescence imaging of biocompatible carbon dots
Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process
One-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence
A ratiometric nanosensor based on fluorescent carbon dots for label-free and highly selective recognition of DNA
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticle modulated turn-on fluorescent probes for histidine detection and its imaging in living cells
Fluorescent and luminescent probes for detection of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species
Ratiometric fluorescent probes to detect reactive oxygen species
Ratiometric fluorescence, electrochemiluminescence, and photoelectrochemical chemo/biosensing based on semiconductor quantum dots
Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations anions, and biomolecules
Visualizing gaseous nitrogendioxide by ratiometric fluorescence of carbon nanodots-quantum dots hybrid
Ratiometric QD-FRET sensing of aqueous H2S in vitro
A gold nanocluster-based fluorescent probe for simultaneous pH and temperature sensing and its application to cellular imaging and logic gates
Iridium (III) complex-coated nanosystem for ratiometric upconversion luminescence bioimaging of cyanide anions
Ratiometric visualization of NO/H2S cross-talk in living cells and tissues using a nitroxyl-responsive two-photon fluorescence probe
Mitochondria-targeted ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for simultaneous biosensing and imaging of O2–and pH in live cells
Ratiometric detection of intracellular lysine and pH with one-pot synthesized dual emissive carbon dots
Cysteine-mediated redox signaling: chemistry, biology, and tools for discovery
Thiol–chromene click chemistry: a coumarin-based derivative and its use as regenerable thiol probe and in bioimaging applications
A gold nanoparticle-based fluorescence sensor for high sensitive and selective detection of thiols in living cells
A simple fluorescent off–on probe for the discrimination of cysteine from glutathione
Recent progress in luminescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of thiols
A highly selective ratiometric near-infrared fluorescent cyanine sensor for cysteine with remarkable shift and its application in bioimaging
Ultrasensitive near-infrared fluorescence-enhanced probe for discriminative detection of GSH and Cys from different emission channels
Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
A sensitive turn-on fluorescent probe for intracellular imaging of glutathione using single-layer MnO2 nanosheet-quenched fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Amino nitrogen quantum dots-Based nanoprobe for fluorescence detection and imaging of cysteine in biological samples
Near-infrared emissive carbon dots for two-photon fluorescence bioimaging
Conformation and activity of glucose oxidase on homogeneously coated and nanostructured surfaces
Amperometric enzyme electrode for urea determination using immobilized urease in poly (vinylferrocenium) film
Red emissive S, N codoped carbon dots and their application in ion detection and theraonostics
Ag nanoparticle-decorated graphene quantum dots for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Ag+ and biothiols

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ya Wang is studying at East China Normal University for her doctor’s degree in analytical chemistry. Her research interest focuses on the synthesis and applications of carbon dots.

Jie Xu is studying at East China Normal University for her master’s degree in analytical chemistry. Her scientific interest focuses on the synthesis of the fluorescence materials for bioimaging and drug delivery.
Li Lei is studying at East China Normal University for her doctor’s degree in analytical chemistry. Her research interest focuses on the preparation and applications of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Fengyang Wang is studying at East China Normal University for her doctor’s degree in analytical chemistry. Her research interest focuses on the synthesis and applications of small molecular fluorescent probes.

Zhiai Xu received her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China, in 2007. Following her doctoral studies, he worked as a JSPS Postdoctoral Fellow at Tohoku University, Japan. Since 2009, she has worked as an assistant Professor at Tohoku University, Japan. She joined Department of Chemistry of East China Normal University in 2012. Her research interests include fluorescent sensors and biosensors, and supramolecular self-assembly of proteins.
Wen Zhang is a Professor at Department of Chemistry of East China Normal University Since 2004. His research interests include electrochemical and fluorescent biosensors.
